title: Subsequent matrimonial unions during subsistence of prior marriage
content: Any individual who, while an active legal marriage remains valid and subsisting, enters into a subsequent matrimonial union that is rendered legally void by reason of the continuation of the prior marriage, faces statutory penalties.

illumination_lamp.scenario: A, who is already legally married to B, goes through a marriage ceremony with C while the marriage with B is still subsisting and valid.
illumination_lamp.legal_effect: A has committed the offence under Section 494.

footnotes.text: The rule against bigamy applies when the personal law governing the individual declares the second union invalid while the first stands. For example, under standard statutory application, it protects the legal monopoly of monogamous unions unless explicitly exempted by valid personal law codes.

title: Matrimonial ceremony fraudulently performed without lawful marriage
content: Whoever dishonestly or with a fraudulent intention goes through the ceremony of marriage, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married, shall be punished.

illumination_lamp.scenario: A, intending to deceive B into believing they are married, arranges a fake ceremony with a person posing as a religious official, knowing full well the ceremony has no legal standing.
illumination_lamp.legal_effect: A is guilty of the offence under Section 496.

footnotes.marker: knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married
footnotes.text: Focuses on deception regarding legal status. This addresses cases where one party is tricked into believing a ceremony creates a binding, lawful state-recognized union when the ceremony itself is legally ineffective or a sham.
